text: Adapted from Natural Surface Trails by Design
by Troy Scott Parker of NatureShape, LLC
Troy Parker suggests that trail managers measure proposed management
actions against the following 12 principles. He maintains that these tests will
quickly separate the good choices from the bad.
I summarize his approach like this:
1. Natural Shapes - like tree roots and branches, streams, and native
stone are pleasing and align with human and natural rhythms. They are
recognizable and attractive at any scale.
2. Anchors - distinct, vertical elements, such as edges and gateways.
They give us a sense that we are here rather than there. We are drawn to
them and feel comfortable with them.
Edges - are extended anchors that we cross quickly, slowly, or not
at all: skirt, follow, cross head-on, cross obliquely, follow edge, or
cross repeatedly. Edges can be natural or man-made.
Gateways - are clearly constrained on two or three sides. The
greater the squeeze, the stronger the gateway experience. We pass
through with surprise, satisfaction, wonder, mystery; establishes a
sense of progress and distance. Can create separation between
the trail world and the car world of the trailhead.
Notes from by Troy Scott Parker Presented by John Monroe, NPS Rivers & Trails Natural Surface Trails by Design
3. Safety - Feelings of safety are measured partly by individual
perception and personality, partly according to actual and perceived
dangers of the situation. Many people desire safety and a certain
amount of risk to challenge their abilities.
4. Efficiency - matters even to people who are not in a hurry: we don’t
want a trail to waste our time. If it does, we create bootleg short-cuts
and by-passes. Users want to travel as fast as the trail, the site and their
mode of travel will allow: design speed trumps speed limits.
5. Playfulness - is the difference between a trail and a road. It’s essential
to recreational trails and includes anticipation, surprise, peacefulness.
Playful trails are highly responsive to the site, with variety and contrasts
in sensory awareness, spaces, rhythm and timing.
6. Harmony - is the overall appropriateness of the trail to the site and
to us. It depends on the context. Like great art, we know it when we see
7. Compaction - is the hammering of the tread by soles, hooves and
wheels causing the material to harden and sink relative to the
surrounding soil. Hardened soil absorbs less water, causing more to run
off. It also makes the tread more resistant to displacement, erosion and
8. Displacement - is the kicking and spraying of particles along and off
� Compaction is limited; displacement is forever.
� Tread materials matter. See Tread Watersheds below.
� On grades, displacement increases and compaction decreases.
� On grades, displace particles creep downhill.
� In muddy or loose tread, compaction can force displacement, too.
� Fast-moving wheels tend to super-elevate curves.
Notes from by Troy Scott Parker Presented by John Monroe, NPS Rivers & Trails Natural Surface Trails by Design
9. Erosion - is the movement of material by water or wind.
We accommodate it; we can’t fight it.
Small erosion events nibble away at the tread.
Erosion starts sooner than you think, within seven feet of a crest.
Small erosion events clog narrow outlets, creating plugs and
Large erosion events are bound to happen and will scour tread
How Erosion, Compaction and Displacement Interact
� Compaction and displacement set the stage for erosion.
� Compacted tread helps to minimize erosion.
� Compaction and displacement can hide evidence of erosion.
� Compaction and displacement can hide evidence of
10. Materials, Texture, Composition & Characteristics - The unique
blend of particle sizes in every soil type determines how it will perform
as a trail tread. Each of the particles below is called a separate. Soil with
more types of separates is more desirable.
11. Tread Watershed - is the tread between a high point (crest) and a
low point (dip) along with the adjacent land area that flows onto this
On existing trails, we can create artificial crests with waterbars.
On new trails we can minimize tread watershed size through
Notes from by Troy Scott Parker Presented by John Monroe, NPS Rivers & Trails Natural Surface Trails by Design
12. Trail Intent - is the process of identifying your objectives for land
and trail management. It is important that this intent (a) is generated
and approved by the board of directors, (b) that it is written down so
it’s easy to implement, and (c) that it guides your day-to-day trail
Troy Parker is currently working up this section of his new book. It will
include a series of challenging questions to help trail managers to
clarify trail intent from a variety of angles.
� How do we accommodate resource protection and visitor
enjoyment? [Note: The staff in every national park faces this
� What is the desired experience for visitors? What will they take
� Can we accomplish our mission with what we’ve got? Can we
really achieve what we want based on on-the-ground realities?
� Are our design principles compatible with our protection
� If we design trails that invite inappropriate behavior and impacts,
are we prepared to arrange for enforcement, or put up with the
